items.definition: Whether the storyboard establishes the real boutique homestay identity early through the gate, sign, facade, reception point, or courtyard entry rather than a context-free styled room.
high_score_signals: Real identity anchors appear within the first three shots
Entrance identity clearly connects to room and public-space footage
Viewers can tell the property is specific and real
low_score_signals: The opening is only mood decor or bedding closeups
Property identity exists only in subtitles
The exterior and interior have no verifiable relationship
items.score_rule: Earlier and clearer identity proof earns a higher score.

items.feature: Cover alignment with the boutique-stay promise
items.definition: Whether the cover direction answers the real reason to choose this boutique homestay, such as arrival switch, host warmth, room pause, local connection, or night mood, instead of generic aesthetic softness.
high_score_signals: Title and frame reinforce the same concrete promise
The cover connects naturally to the first shots
The reason to stay here is obvious at a glance
low_score_signals: The cover is only decor, flowers, bedding, or candles
The copy promise and cover proof do not match
The cover promises luxury while the assets only support a simple small-property route
items.score_rule: The more clearly the cover answers why this property matters, the higher the score.

items.definition: Whether the storyboard stays anchored to a real bookable hero room and proves bed, window or terrace, table surface, bathroom, and everyday usability rather than just magazine-style composition.
high_score_signals: One room type stays continuous and legible
Bed, bathroom, window, and table relationships are clear
Promised room features such as tubs or terraces are directly shown
low_score_signals: Multiple room types are mixed into one fake hero room
Only beautiful corners appear with no practical layout proof
Aesthetic emphasis hides actual stay conditions
items.score_rule: More honest and more usable room proof earns a higher score.

items.definition: When the script sells warmth, whether the storyboard includes real host, manager, or service traces such as welcome notes, key handoff, or breakfast prep rather than abstract claims.
high_score_signals: Real receiving or host-led service moments appear
Host proof connects to room or breakfast flow
Warmth is shown without violating privacy boundaries
low_score_signals: Warmth depends on actors or generic smiles
The host promise exists only in copy
Third-party service is misframed as in-house host care
items.score_rule: The more concrete and authorized the service evidence, the higher the score.

items.definition: Whether claims about old streets, riversides, villages, markets, or art districts are grounded in a real route relationship to the property entrance.
high_score_signals: The entrance and local anchor share a believable route link
Arrival or return movement is understandable
Location promises map directly to shootable proof
low_score_signals: Only pretty local scenery appears
Maps replace real route footage
Walkable claims have no physical anchor
items.score_rule: More specific, more verifiable location proof earns a higher score.

items.definition: Whether tea rooms, rooftops, courtyards, terraces, and other public areas are shown as real on-site spaces with visible ownership, scale, and access boundaries.
high_score_signals: Whole-space coverage shows scale and relation
Ownership and open state are clear
The public-space claim matches the property type and guest scene
low_score_signals: Only isolated detail shots appear
Nearby third-party spaces stand in for property assets
Access and ownership remain ambiguous
items.score_rule: More complete and more attributable public-space proof earns a higher score.

items.definition: Whether mood, season, weather, and night conditions match what is actually available to shoot, and whether the plan downgrades honestly when those conditions are missing.
high_score_signals: Atmosphere matches real weather and time windows
Night routes include lighting and return logic
Cloudy or rainy conditions trigger a believable indoor pivot
low_score_signals: Weather, season, or time cues contradict each other
Night mood is promised without night coverage
Stock or heavily manipulated condition shots drive the mood
items.score_rule: The more the mood comes from real conditions and honest downgrade choices, the higher the score.

items.definition: Whether the storyboard creates a believable boutique homestay stay flow from arrival to room pause to breakfast or departure rather than a loose collection of pretty scenes.
high_score_signals: At least three connected stay moments form a clear chain
Time flow between arrival, stay, and closure is coherent
The viewer can imagine a real one-night stay
low_score_signals: Scenes are only static room or public-space displays
Day, night, and morning are cut together with no logic
The chain depends on many missing assets or fake people
items.score_rule: More coherent stay logic under real operations earns a higher score.

items.feature: Dependency honesty for a small property
items.definition: Whether the plan clearly lists room access, host availability, breakfast windows, night permission, guest privacy boundaries, and fallback routes instead of hiding operational risk.
high_score_signals: Each key shot maps to a real asset, time, or permission
Fallback paths and lowered ambition are explicit
No imagined or external scenes are treated as executable proof
low_score_signals: Dependencies are vague or generic
Feasibility stays high despite large asset gaps
Risk is pushed downstream without downgrade logic
items.score_rule: More specific dependencies and more honest downgrade behavior earn a higher score.
